fault.fr: Pâte collante et impossible à travailler

signs: Dough clings to hands, bowl and counter and won't hold together
Feels more like batter than dough
causes: Underdeveloped gluten (far more often the real cause than water)
Dough too warm, which always feels stickier at a given hydration
Hydration genuinely too high for the flour's strength
fixes: Autolyse 30-60 minutes before final mixing so flour fully hydrates first
Use wet hands or a bench scraper with Rubaud or slap-and-fold instead of adding flour
Chill the dough briefly if it's simply too warm
Only as a last resort, lower hydration by 2-3% at a time

fault.de: Teig reißt, besteht den Fenstertest nicht
fault.es: La masa se rasga, no pasa la prueba de la ventana
fault.fr: La pâte se déchire, pas de voile au test de la vitre

signs: Tears rather than stretching thin when tested
Can't reach a thin, translucent windowpane without ripping
causes: Under-mixed - not enough kneading or folding yet
Low-protein or bran-heavy flour cutting the gluten strands
Dough is early in a long, cold-fermentation schedule where full development isn't expected yet
fixes: Continue mixing or add more stretch-and-fold sets, then retest
Blend in a stronger bread or high-gluten flour, or reduce whole-grain percentage
If the dough is meant for a long bulk rise, confirm only partial development is needed now - strength will build on its own

fault.es: Miga que se desmorona y reseca

signs: Slices fall apart or crumble when cut
Crumb feels dry and short rather than moist and cohesive
causes: Dough mixed too stiff or low-hydration for the flour used
High proportion of low-gluten grain (whole wheat, rye, ancient grains) without adjusting technique
Overbaked, driving off more moisture than the crumb can spare
fixes: Raise hydration slightly, judged against how the dough actually feels, not just the percentage
Add an autolyse or soaker for whole-grain portions so bran fully hydrates before mixing
Pull the bake at the correct internal temperature rather than over-baking for color

signs: Tight, compact crumb with small, uniform air pockets
Heavy for its size
Little to no oven spring
causes: Bulk fermentation ended too early, before the dough gained real volume
Weak or young starter, or insufficient yeast
Dough temperature too cold during bulk
fixes: Let bulk fermentation run until the dough visibly grows (roughly 50% by volume), not by the clock
Track rise with a straight-sided container or aliquot jar instead of guessing
Warm the dough (24-26°C) to speed a sluggish fermentation

signs: Spreads outward rather than rising upward
Minimal oven spring
Dough flattened noticeably when turned out of the banneton
causes: Overproofed - fermentation ran past the dough's structural limit
Underdeveloped gluten too weak to hold gas
Shaping left too little surface tension
fixes: End bulk fermentation earlier (roughly 25-75% rise depending on flour strength), judged by feel, not doubling
Build more strength with extra stretch-and-folds
Reshape with firmer, more deliberate tension

signs: Sharp, vinegary, almost acetone-like tang
Sourness overpowers the wheat or rye flavor
causes: Long, cool fermentation favoring heterofermentative (acetic-acid) bacteria
Overripe or infrequently-fed starter
Too much prefermented flour or too long a cold retard
fixes: Ferment warmer (around 28-30°C) to favor mild lactic acid bacteria over acetic
Feed the starter more often and use it closer to its peak
Shorten bulk fermentation or reduce the amount of levain used

signs: Flat, mild, more yeasty than tangy flavor
Little of the expected sourdough character
causes: Short or warm fermentation dominated by yeast rather than acid-producing bacteria
Young, recently-fed starter with a high yeast-to-bacteria ratio
Low percentage of prefermented flour
fixes: Extend bulk fermentation, including a cold retard, to build more acid
Let the starter ripen longer between feeds, or feed it less often
Increase prefermented flour percentage, or use a stiffer levain, which favors sharper acetic notes

fault.es: Miga cerrada pese a una alta hidratación
fault.fr: Mie serrée malgré une forte hydratation

signs: Crumb stays close and tight even though the dough felt very wet and slack
Doesn't match the open crumb the hydration percentage would suggest
causes: Bulk fermentation ended too soon - hydration alone doesn't open a crumb, fermentation does
Overly sour or degraded starter weakening gas production
Whole grain or rye flour in the mix, which tightens crumb regardless of water level
Dough handled too roughly, popping gas pockets built during bulk
fixes: Extend bulk fermentation and judge by volume and feel rather than the hydration number
Refresh the starter until it reliably peaks before using it
Handle gently at shaping - firm but not aggressive degassing

signs: Small raised bubbles or blisters across the crust surface
Blisters sometimes brown darker than the surrounding crust
causes: Over-fermented or over-acidified dough
Dough soft, cold or under-proofed going into a hot oven, trapping surface gas unevenly
Enzyme-rich flour breaking down surface starch unevenly
fixes: Rein in fermentation time or temperature to avoid over-acidification
Bring dough to a consistent, appropriate temperature before baking
Handle gently at shaping to avoid uneven surface gas pockets

signs: One or a few large irregular holes rather than an even crumb
Holes often run just under the crust
causes: Excess dusting flour trapped inside the shaping seam, preventing it from sealing
Rough degassing or shaping that left large trapped pockets
Underfermented dough that then expanded unevenly and fast in the oven
fixes: Brush off excess bench flour before the final shaping fold
Shape with even, deliberate pressure so gas is distributed rather than pushed into pockets
Let fermentation finish properly before shaping

signs: Score simply splits open flat with no raised, curled lip
The cut looks splayed rather than lifted
causes: Blade held perpendicular (90°) instead of shallow (~30°) to the dough
Cut too shallow to release before the crust sets, or too deep so it collapses instead of lifting
Slow, dragging cut instead of one fast, confident pass
Insufficient steam, so the crust sets before the score can open
fixes: Angle the lame or blade to about 30 degrees and cut in one swift motion
Cut 1/4 to 1/2 inch deep along the length of the loaf
Make sure steam is present for the first 10-15 minutes of baking

signs: Loaf splits open along an unplanned line, often on the side or bottom
The rupture follows the shaping seam rather than the score
causes: Seam not fully sealed during shaping
Dough underproofed, so it still had a lot of oven-rise left to do
Insufficient steam let the crust set before expansion finished
fixes: Pinch and seal the seam firmly, then proof with the seam positioned so it isn't the structural weak point
Let final proof go further before baking
Increase steam for the first part of the bake

signs: Wet, unset, tacky crumb even after full cooling
Crumb sticks to the knife when sliced
causes: Sliced before fully cooled - starch is still setting
Underbaked, pulled before the core reached roughly 96-99°C
Severe overfermentation broke down starch and gluten before baking
fixes: Cool at least 1-2 hours (longer for large or rye loaves) before the first cut
Verify doneness with an instant-read thermometer, not crust color alone
Shorten bulk or final fermentation next time if the loaf is fully baked and still gummy

signs: Crust stays light tan or blond even after full bake time
Little shine or color contrast
causes: Oven temperature too low or inaccurate
Too much residual steam left in for too long
Overfermented dough, leaving little residual sugar for browning
Underbaked, pulled before Maillard and caramelization had time to develop
fixes: Verify actual oven temperature with a separate thermometer
Vent steam earlier so the back half of the bake can brown
Shorten fermentation slightly to preserve sugars for crust color
Extend bake time rather than judging doneness by the clock alone

signs: Top crust separates into a hollow dome over an air gap
Crust and crumb pull apart, sometimes visible on slicing
causes: Large trapped gas pocket just under the skin from insufficient degassing at shaping
Oven too hot, setting the top crust before the interior finished expanding
Weak scoring that gave expanding gas nowhere else to go
fixes: Degas a little more evenly during shaping to avoid one large subsurface bubble
Confirm oven temperature and consider baking slightly cooler
Score deliberately so expansion has a controlled release point

signs: Loaf rises then visibly sinks or deflates once in the oven
Sides can look pinched in or wrinkled
causes: Overproofed before baking - yeast activity exhausted, no reserve left for oven spring
Weak gluten network (under-mixed or over-fermented) that can't hold structure once heated
Rough handling into the oven collapsed built-up gas cells
fixes: Use the poke test (a finger dent springs back slowly, not instantly, not left sunken) instead of a fixed time
Build more dough strength through mixing and folds
Load the loaf gently and quickly, minimizing jostling

fault.es: "Ataque del almidón" en pan de centeno

signs: Dense, pasty, gummy crumb specific to rye loaves
Crumb feels wet and heavy even when fully baked
causes: High-diastatic (enzyme-rich) rye flour with strong amylase activity
Insufficient dough acidity to suppress amylase before baking
Baking too slowly through the roughly 50-60°C range, giving enzymes more time to break down starch before heat deactivates them near 80°C
fixes: Use a well-soured, mature rye sourdough to acidify the dough and inhibit amylase
Increase the proportion of prefermented, soured rye flour in the formula
Bake at the specified full temperature rather than a slow ramp, crossing the enzyme-active window quickly

signs: Crust looks done but crumb near the center is wet, dense or doughy
Loaf feels heavier than expected once cool
causes: Pulled from the oven based on crust color alone, especially with a bold, dark bake
Oven running hotter than the dial, over-browning the outside while the inside lags
Loaf too large or too wet for the bake time given
fixes: Confirm doneness with an instant-read thermometer - about 96-99°C at the center for lean hearth breads
Tent with foil if the crust colors faster than the core cooks
Extend bake time and/or lower the temperature slightly for large or high-hydration loaves

signs: Crust is hard and difficult to bite through rather than crisp and shattering
Sometimes rubbery instead of hard
causes: Too little steam in the first 10-15 minutes, so the crust set and thickened too early
Overbaked past the target internal temperature
Oven actually cooler than set, requiring a longer bake that over-dries the surface
fixes: Increase steam at the start of the bake (more water, cover longer, or add a preheated steaming pan)
Pull the loaf at the correct internal temperature rather than by time alone
Verify true oven temperature and adjust

signs: Crust looks flat and chalky rather than glossy
Lacks the glassy sheen of a well-steamed loaf
causes: Surface dried out before baking (proofed uncovered too long)
Overfermented dough with too little surface sugar left for a glossy set
Excessive top heat drying the surface before steam could gelatinize it
fixes: Cover the dough during final proof to keep the surface from drying
Ease back fermentation time slightly
Ensure steam reaches the loaf's surface early in the bake, before strong top heat sets it

signs: Crumb feels firm, dry or tough compared to the bake day
Bread eats dry or dense even though nothing looks obviously wrong
causes: Starch retrogradation - amylose and amylopectin recrystallizing as the loaf cools and sits, not primarily water loss
Storage at refrigerator temperature, which accelerates retrogradation versus room temperature
Lean, low-fat formula with nothing to slow the process
fixes: Store at room temperature, cut-side down or well wrapped, never in the fridge
Freeze sliced bread if it won't be eaten within a couple of days - freezing halts staling far better than the fridge
Refresh by toasting or a few minutes in a hot oven, which re-gelatinizes surface starch

fault.es: La corteza se ablanda de un día para otro
fault.fr: Croûte ramollie du jour au lendemain

signs: A crackly-crisp crust turns leathery or soft within hours of cooling
Crust loses its crackle even though the loaf hasn't otherwise staled
causes: Moisture migrating from the moist interior crumb into the drier crust as the loaf sits
Storing in a sealed plastic bag while the crust is still warm or moist
Humid storage environment
fixes: Cool completely on a rack before wrapping so trapped steam can escape first
Store crusty breads in paper or a breathable bread bag rather than sealed plastic, if crust crispness matters more than crumb moisture
Re-crisp in a hot oven for a few minutes before serving
